name: Hemoglobin
description: Hemoglobin is the iron-containing oxygen-transport metalloprotein in red blood cells responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues.
clinical_use: Used to diagnose and monitor anemia, polycythemia, acute or chronic blood loss, and response to hematologic treatments.

ranges.label: Neonate normal
ranges.note: Values are highest at birth (up to 240 g/L) and decline rapidly in the first weeks of life.

ranges.label: Infant normal
ranges.note: Reflects the physiologic nadir occurring between 2 and 6 months of age.

references.source: CALIPER Project (Canadian Laboratory Initiative on Pediatric Reference Intervals)
WHO: Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia
British Journal of Haematology: Reference intervals for a pediatric population
WHO: Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia
WHO Vitamin and Mineral Nutrition Information System
CLSI EP28-A3c: Defining, Establishing, and Verifying Reference Intervals
WHO Vitamin and Mineral Nutrition Information System

critical_values.meaning: Severe anemia; high risk of cardiopulmonary compromise, consider immediate transfusion
Severe polycythemia; risk of hyperviscosity syndrome and thrombosis

warnings: Reference ranges are physiologically higher in populations living at high altitudes and in heavy smokers.
Hemodilution during the second and third trimesters of pregnancy naturally lowers hemoglobin concentrations.
Severe dehydration can cause falsely elevated hemoglobin levels.
Pediatric ranges are highly age-dependent; ensure exact age is used for interpretation.
notes: The physiologic nadir in infancy occurs around 8-12 weeks as fetal hemoglobin (HbF) is replaced by adult hemoglobin (HbA).
Neonatal values vary significantly based on timing of cord clamping and gestational age.

references.title: Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia and assessment of severity
references.journal: WHO Vitamin and Mineral Nutrition Information System

references.title: Closing the gaps in pediatric laboratory reference intervals: a CALIPER database of 40 biochemical markers in a healthy and multiethnic population of children
